message:
feat(pool): add horizontal sharding infrastructure

Introduces ShardedPool for distributing data across multiple database shards:

New sharding.rs module:
- ShardChooser trait: Strategy interface for shard selection
- choose_for_model(): Route writes based on model's shard key value
- choose_for_query(): Select shards for read queries with hints
- QueryHints: Metadata for intelligent shard selection

ShardedPool implementation:
- add_shard(name, pool): Register named shard connection pools
- Execute operations against correct shard based on model shard key
- Parallel scatter-gather for cross-shard queries

Example ShardChooser implementations documented:
- ModuloShardChooser: Hash-based distribution (user_id % shard_count)
- RangeShardChooser: Range-based partitioning
- ConsistentHashChooser: Stable distribution with minimal resharding

create.rs schema additions:
- Support for sharded table DDL generation
- Shard key validation in schema definitions

This pairs with the Model::SHARD_KEY trait constant to enable
transparent sharding at the ORM layer.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -18,7 +18,7 @@
 {"id":"bd-1ak","title":"EPIC: ORM Relationship System","description":"# ORM Relationship System

## Overview
Implement full ORM relationship support comparable to SQLAlchemy/SQLModel. This is THE defining feature that separates an ORM from a mere query builder with type mapping.

## Why This Matters
Without relationships, users must:
- Manually write JOINs for every related query
- Manually track foreign keys
- Lose the core ergonomic benefit of an ORM (`hero.team` should just work)

Python SQLModel provides:
```python
class Hero(SQLModel, table=True):
    team_id: Optional[int] = Field(foreign_key="team.id")
    team: Optional["Team"] = Relationship(back_populates="heroes")

class Team(SQLModel, table=True):
    heroes: List["Hero"] = Relationship(back_populates="team")

# Usage: hero.team automatically fetches the related Team
```

## Rust Design Philosophy
Instead of runtime reflection magic, we use:
1. **Compile-time type safety** via proc macros
2. **Explicit relationship definitions** that generate correct code
3. **Optional lazy loading** (user opts in, not default)
4. **Eager loading support** for performance-conscious queries

## Target API (Rust)
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "heroes")]
struct Hero {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(foreign_key = "teams.id")]
    team_id: Option<i64>,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Team", foreign_key = "team_id"))]
    team: Related<Team>,  // or Lazy<Team> for lazy loading
}

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table = "teams")]
struct Team {
    #[sqlmodel(primary_key, auto_increment)]
    id: Option<i64>,
    
    name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(relationship(model = "Hero", back_populates = "team"))]
    heroes: RelatedMany<Hero>,
}
```

## Success Criteria
- [ ] One-to-many relationships work (Team has many Heroes)
- [ ] Many-to-one relationships work (Hero belongs to Team)
- [ ] Many-to-many with link tables work
- [ ] Eager loading with JOINs works
- [ ] Optional lazy loading works
- [ ] back_populates synchronization works
- [ ] Cascade operations work via FK constraints
- [ ] Type-safe at compile time

## Architecture Decisions
1. **Related<T> vs Lazy<T>**: Two wrapper types - Related for eager-loaded, Lazy for deferred
2. **No automatic lazy loading by default**: Explicit is better for Rust, prevents N+1 surprises
3. **Macro-generated accessors**: The relationship macro generates the fetch methods
4. **Connection context required**: Must pass connection/session to load related data

## Dependencies
- Core Model trait (done)
- Query builder with JOINs (done)
- Session/Unit-of-Work (separate epic, can work without but better with)

## Estimated Scope
- 8-12 subtasks
- Touches: sqlmodel-core, sqlmodel-macros, sqlmodel-query
- New types: Related<T>, RelatedMany<T>, Lazy<T>, RelationshipInfo","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-27T20:12:54.238244618Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379475967Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T00:04:07.379414012Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-1bt4","title":"Write comprehensive conversion boundary tests for Value type safety","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:16.270980935Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T01:32:10.443761460Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T01:32:10.443680780Z","close_reason":"Added 17 comprehensive conversion boundary tests covering u64, f32, f64 precision, error messages, roundtrips, and negative boundaries","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","depends_on_id":"bd-1epw","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:17.564472424Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","depends_on_id":"bd-21wb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:18.863992793Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","depends_on_id":"bd-31ed","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:18.203169124Z","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","depends_on_id":"bd-9a25","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:16.277373714Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":62,"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## What

Write comprehensive unit tests verifying that all numeric Value conversions either succeed correctly or fail with clear errors — covering the u64 overflow fix, f32 precision fix, and i64→f64 precision fix.

## Where

File: crates/sqlmodel-core/src/value.rs (extend existing test module)
File: crates/sqlmodel-core/src/row.rs (extend existing test module)

## Unit Tests Required

### u64 Overflow Tests (value.rs)
1. `test_u64_zero_converts` — `0u64.try_into::<Value>()` → Ok(BigInt(0))
2. `test_u64_max_i64_converts` — `(i64::MAX as u64).try_into()` → Ok(BigInt(i64::MAX))
3. `test_u64_over_i64_max_errors` — `(i64::MAX as u64 + 1).try_into()` → Err with clear message
4. `test_u64_max_errors` — `u64::MAX.try_into()` → Err
5. `test_u64_error_message_includes_value` — verify the error message contains the actual u64 value for debugging
6. `test_u64_boundary_i64_max_minus_one` — `(i64::MAX as u64 - 1).try_into()` → Ok

### f32 Precision Tests (value.rs)
7. `test_f64_to_f32_small_value_ok` — `Value::Double(1.5).try_into::<f32>()` → Ok(1.5)
8. `test_f64_to_f32_zero_ok` — `Value::Double(0.0).try_into::<f32>()` → Ok(0.0)
9. `test_f64_to_f32_large_value_errors` — `Value::Double(f64::MAX).try_into::<f32>()` → Err
10. `test_f64_to_f32_precision_loss_errors` — `Value::Double(1.0000000000000002).try_into::<f32>()` → Err (if precision lost)
11. `test_i32_to_f32_small_ok` — `Value::Int(1000).try_into::<f32>()` → Ok(1000.0)
12. `test_i32_to_f32_exact_boundary_ok` — `Value::Int(16_777_216).try_into::<f32>()` → Ok (2^24 is exact)
13. `test_i32_to_f32_over_boundary_errors` — `Value::Int(16_777_217).try_into::<f32>()` → Err (2^24+1 loses precision)
14. `test_i64_to_f32_large_errors` — `Value::BigInt(i64::MAX).try_into::<f32>()` → Err
15. `test_f32_error_message_is_descriptive` — verify error includes the value and explains precision loss

### i64→f64 Precision Tests (value.rs)
16. `test_i64_to_f64_small_ok` — `Value::BigInt(42).try_into::<f64>()` → Ok(42.0)
17. `test_i64_to_f64_exact_boundary_ok` — `Value::BigInt(1i64 << 53).try_into::<f64>()` → Ok (2^53 is exact in f64)
18. `test_i64_to_f64_over_boundary_errors` — `Value::BigInt((1i64 << 53) + 1).try_into::<f64>()` → Err
19. `test_i64_to_f64_negative_boundary_ok` — `Value::BigInt(-(1i64 << 53)).try_into::<f64>()` → Ok
20. `test_i64_to_f64_negative_over_boundary_errors` — `Value::BigInt(-(1i64 << 53) - 1).try_into::<f64>()` → Err
21. `test_i64_to_f64_max_errors` — `Value::BigInt(i64::MAX).try_into::<f64>()` → Err
22. `test_i64_to_f64_min_errors` — `Value::BigInt(i64::MIN).try_into::<f64>()` → Err (MIN = -2^63, exceeds 2^53)

### Row FromValue Tests (row.rs — mirrors value.rs tests)
23. `test_row_f32_from_large_double_errors` — verify FromValue for f32 from a Double row value errors
24. `test_row_f32_from_small_double_ok` — verify small values work
25. `test_row_f64_from_large_bigint_errors` — verify FromValue for f64 from a BigInt row value errors
26. `test_row_f64_from_small_bigint_ok` — verify small values work

### Roundtrip Tests
27. `test_u64_roundtrip_within_range` — u64 → Value → i64 → verify equal for values in range
28. `test_f32_roundtrip_preserves_value` — f32 → Value::Float → f32 → verify bitwise equal
29. `test_f64_i64_roundtrip` — i64 (small) → Value → f64 → verify equal

### Error Type Tests
30. `test_conversion_errors_are_type_errors` — verify all conversion failures return Error::Type variant
31. `test_conversion_errors_include_expected_type` — verify error has the target type name
32. `test_conversion_errors_include_actual_value` — verify error has the source value description

## Logging / Diagnostics

Each test should use descriptive assertion messages:
```rust
assert!(result.is_err(), "u64::MAX ({}) should not silently convert to i64", u64::MAX);
let err = result.unwrap_err();
assert!(
    err.to_string().contains("18446744073709551615"),
    "Error message should include the actual value, got: {}",
    err
);
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- At least 30 tests covering all conversion paths and boundary conditions
- Every error path tested with message content verification
- row.rs and value.rs both covered
- Tests document WHY each boundary value matters (comments)
- All tests pass with `cargo test -p sqlmodel-core`
- cargo clippy clean
","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:35:16Z"},{"id":75,"issue_id":"bd-1bt4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## UPDATE: u64 tests must reflect corrected approach (see bd-1epw latest comment)

Since From<u64> is being REMOVED (not kept), the u64 tests should be:

### u64 Strict Path (TryFrom)
1. `test_u64_zero_try_into` — `0u64.try_into::<Value>()` → Ok(BigInt(0))
2. `test_u64_max_i64_try_into` — `(i64::MAX as u64).try_into()` → Ok(BigInt(i64::MAX))
3. `test_u64_over_i64_max_try_into_errors` — `(i64::MAX as u64 + 1).try_into()` → Err
4. `test_u64_max_try_into_errors` — `u64::MAX.try_into()` → Err
5. `test_u64_error_message_includes_value` — verify error contains the actual u64 value
6. `test_u64_boundary_minus_one` — `(i64::MAX as u64 - 1).try_into()` → Ok

### u64 Lenient Path (from_u64_clamped)
7. `test_u64_clamped_in_range` — `Value::from_u64_clamped(42)` → BigInt(42)
8. `test_u64_clamped_over_max` — `Value::from_u64_clamped(u64::MAX)` → BigInt(i64::MAX)
9. `test_u64_clamped_at_boundary` — `Value::from_u64_clamped(i64::MAX as u64 + 1)` → BigInt(i64::MAX)

### Reverse path (Value → u64)
10. `test_negative_bigint_to_u64_errors` — `Value::BigInt(-1).try_into::<u64>()` → Err
11. `test_zero_bigint_to_u64` — `Value::BigInt(0).try_into::<u64>()` → Ok(0)

Replace original tests 1-6 with these 11 tests.

 {"id":"bd-1cr","title":"Field Attributes: Complete All 40+ Parameters","description":"## Overview

Implement ALL Field() parameters from Python SQLModel/Pydantic.

## Python Field() Parameters (Complete List)

### Basic Parameters
- default: Any - Default value for field
- default_factory: Optional[Callable] - Factory function for defaults
- alias: Optional[str] - Input/output alias
- validation_alias: Optional[str] - Input-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- serialization_alias: Optional[str] - Output-only alias (Pydantic v2)
- title: Optional[str] - Field title in schema
- description: Optional[str] - Field description in schema
- exclude: Union[set, Mapping] - Exclude from model operations
- include: Union[set, Mapping] - Include in model operations
- schema_extra: Optional[dict] - Extra schema configuration
- const: Optional[bool] - Constant value constraint
- repr: bool - Include in repr (default: True)

### Validation Parameters
- gt, ge, lt, le: Optional[float] - Numeric bounds
- multiple_of: Optional[float] - Must be multiple of value
- max_digits: Optional[int] - Max decimal digits
- decimal_places: Optional[int] - Decimal precision
- min_items, max_items: Optional[int] - Collection size bounds
- unique_items: Optional[bool] - Items must be unique
- min_length, max_length: Optional[int] - String length bounds
- allow_mutation: bool - Allow field mutation
- regex: Optional[str] - Regex pattern validation
- discriminator: Optional[str] - Union type discrimination

### SQLAlchemy Column Parameters
- primary_key, nullable, unique, index - Column constraints
- sa_type - SQLAlchemy type override
- sa_column - Full SQLAlchemy Column (mutually exclusive with other SA params)
- sa_column_args, sa_column_kwargs - Extra Column() args/kwargs
- foreign_key, ondelete, onupdate - Foreign key config

## Current Status

Many are implemented but gaps exist in:
- Alias system (validation_alias, serialization_alias)
- Schema metadata (title, description, schema_extra)
- Collection validators (min_items, max_items, unique_items)
- Full sa_column override support
- multiple_of validation
- discriminator support

## Implementation Notes

Each parameter must:
1. Be parseable from #[sqlmodel(...)] or #[field(...)] attributes
2. Generate appropriate SQL DDL where applicable
3. Generate validation code where applicable
4. Work with serde for serialization
5. Have tests covering all edge cases","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-28T04:59:11.095624519Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T13:02:22.502871496Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T13:02:22.502805383Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1cr","depends_on_id":"bd-162","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:58:19.317440856Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
-{"id":"bd-1cuq","title":"Implement horizontal sharding","description":"## Description

Partition data across multiple databases.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy.ext.horizontal_shard import ShardedSession

class ShardedDB:
    def shard_chooser(self, mapper, instance, clause=None):
        if instance:
            return f'shard_{instance.region}'
        return 'shard_default'
    
    def id_chooser(self, query, ident):
        return ['shard_us', 'shard_eu']  # Search all
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
pub struct ShardedPool {
    shards: HashMap<String, Pool>,
    chooser: Box<dyn ShardChooser>,
}

pub trait ShardChooser: Send + Sync {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String;
    fn choose_for_query(&self, query: &Query) -> Vec<String>;
}

// Example: shard by user_id modulo
struct ModuloShardChooser {
    shard_count: usize,
}

impl ShardChooser for ModuloShardChooser {
    fn choose_for_insert<M: Model>(&self, model: &M) -> String {
        let id = model.shard_key();
        format!("shard_{}", id % self.shard_count)
    }
}
```

## Shard Key

Models need shard key:
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(shard_key = "user_id")]
struct Order {
    user_id: i64,
    // ...
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ShardedPool implementation
- [ ] ShardChooser trait
- [ ] Shard key on models
- [ ] Cross-shard queries
- [ ] Shard-aware transactions

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/sharding.rs)
- [ ] Test shard key extraction
- [ ] Test shard routing function
- [ ] Test cross-shard query handling
- [ ] Test shard configuration

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/horizontal_sharding.rs)
- [ ] Query routes to correct shard
- [ ] Insert routes by shard key
- [ ] Cross-shard aggregation
- [ ] Shard rebalancing
- [ ] Shard failover

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Shard routing decision
- [ ] INFO: Shard selection
- [ ] WARN: Cross-shard query detected

 {"id":"bd-1cy","title":"Implement Window Functions (OVER clause)","description":"## Description

Support window functions with OVER clause.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy import func, over

# Row number
stmt = select(
    User.name,
    func.row_number().over(order_by=User.created_at).label('row_num')
)

# Partition by
stmt = select(
    Order.id,
    func.sum(Order.amount).over(partition_by=Order.customer_id).label('customer_total')
)

# Window frame
stmt = select(
    Sale.date,
    func.avg(Sale.amount).over(
        partition_by=Sale.product_id,
        order_by=Sale.date,
        rows=(-2, 0)  # Current row and 2 preceding
    )
)
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
let query = select!(User)
    .column(Expr::function("row_number", vec![])
        .over()
        .order_by(User::created_at.asc())
        .as_("row_num")
    );

let query = select!(Order)
    .column(Order::amount.sum()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Order::customer_id)
        .as_("customer_total")
    );

// With frame
let query = select!(Sale)
    .column(Sale::amount.avg()
        .over()
        .partition_by(Sale::product_id)
        .order_by(Sale::date.asc())
        .rows_between(-2, 0)  // ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW
    );
```

## Window Functions to Support

- ROW_NUMBER()
- RANK()
- DENSE_RANK()
- NTILE(n)
- LAG(expr, offset)
- LEAD(expr, offset)
- FIRST_VALUE(expr)
- LAST_VALUE(expr)
- NTH_VALUE(expr, n)

Plus aggregates with OVER:
- SUM(), AVG(), COUNT(), MIN(), MAX()

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] over() creates window function
- [ ] partition_by() works
- [ ] order_by() works
- [ ] rows_between() for frame
- [ ] range_between() for frame
- [ ] All window functions supported
- [ ] Correct SQL for each dialect

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-query/src/window.rs)
- [ ] Test ROW_NUMBER() OVER generation
- [ ] Test RANK() and DENSE_RANK()
- [ ] Test PARTITION BY clause
- [ ] Test ORDER BY within OVER
- [ ] Test frame specification (ROWS/RANGE)
- [ ] Test LAG/LEAD functions
- [ ] Test aggregate functions in OVER

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/query_window.rs)
- [ ] SELECT with ROW_NUMBER() ranks correctly
- [ ] PARTITION BY groups correctly
- [ ] Running sum with SUM() OVER
- [ ] Complex window: RANK() PARTITION BY x ORDER BY y
- [ ] Multiple window functions in same query
- [ ] Window function in subquery

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Window clause SQL generation
- [ ] TRACE: Frame specification details
","notes":"DarkStone working on implementing window functions. Starting with core Window struct and OVER clause support.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:07:06.311240310Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T18:18:30.964955236Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T18:18:30.964888201Z","close_reason":"Implemented window functions with OVER clause support. Added: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, DENSE_RANK, PERCENT_RANK, CUME_DIST, NTILE, LAG, LEAD, FIRST_VALUE, LAST_VALUE, NTH_VALUE. Also added PARTITION BY, ORDER BY within windows, and frame specifications (ROWS/RANGE/GROUPS BETWEEN). All 21 window function tests pass.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1cy","depends_on_id":"bd-1n7","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:57:09.029437018Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"bd-1dv","title":"Implement field alias system (alias, validation_alias, serialization_alias)","description":"## Description

Add support for field aliases matching Pydantic behavior.

## Python Behavior

```python
class User(SQLModel):
    internal_id: int = Field(alias='id')
    user_name: str = Field(
        validation_alias='username',  # Accept 'username' on input
        serialization_alias='userName'  # Output as 'userName'
    )
```

## Rust Implementation

### Macro Attributes
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    #[sqlmodel(alias = "id")]
    internal_id: i32,
    
    #[sqlmodel(
        validation_alias = "username",
        serialization_alias = "userName"
    )]
    user_name: String,
}
```

### Generated Code

For validation_alias:
- Accept the alias during deserialization
- Map to actual field name

For serialization_alias:

### Serde Integration

Use #[serde(alias = "...")] for validation_alias
Use #[serde(rename = "...")] for serialization_alias

- crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/model.rs - Generate serde attributes
- crates/sqlmodel/tests/alias.rs - Add tests

- [ ] validation_alias only affects input
- [ ] serialization_alias only affects output

- [ ] validation_alias + serialization_alias asymmetric
- [ ] Database column uses serialization_alias
- [ ] API round-trip with aliases

### Logging

- [ ] TRACE: Field name mapping decisions

 {"id":"bd-1epw","title":"Fix From<u64> for Value — prevent silent overflow to negative i64","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:00.982271890Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T01:23:01.193554484Z","closed_at":"2026-01-30T01:23:01.193490795Z","close_reason":"Removed From<u64>, added TryFrom<u64> with overflow error, added Value::from_u64_clamped() with warning on clamp","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-1epw","depends_on_id":"bd-9a25","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:00.988294841Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":53,"issue_id":"bd-1epw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## What

Fix the `From<u64> for Value` implementation that silently wraps values > i64::MAX.

## Where

File: crates/sqlmodel-core/src/value.rs, line 251-256

## Current Code

```rust
impl From<u64> for Value {
    fn from(v: u64) -> Self {
        // May overflow for very large values, but best effort
        Value::BigInt(v as i64)
    }
}
```

## Options

### Option A: Change From<u64> to TryFrom<u64> (RECOMMENDED)
```rust
impl TryFrom<u64> for Value {
    type Error = Error;
    fn try_from(v: u64) -> Result<Self, Self::Error> {
        if v > i64::MAX as u64 {
            Err(Error::Type(TypeError {
                expected: "i64-representable u64",
                actual: format!("u64 value {} exceeds i64::MAX", v),
                column: None,
                rust_type: None,
            }))
        } else {
            Ok(Value::BigInt(v as i64))
        }
    }
}
```

Pros: No new Value variant, clear error on overflow
Cons: Breaks callers that use `.into()` on u64 — they need `.try_into()?`

### Option B: Add Value::UBigInt(u64) variant
Pros: Lossless, preserves From impl
Cons: Every match on Value needs a new arm; SQL generation needs CAST; drivers need new handling

### Recommendation: Option A

TryFrom is the Rust-idiomatic approach. The breakage is small (grep for `u64` usage in Value contexts) and the error message is clear.

## Acceptance Criteria

- u64 values > i64::MAX produce an error, not silent corruption
- All existing tests pass (may need updating)
- New test: `u64::MAX.try_into()` returns Err
- New test: `(i64::MAX as u64).try_into()` returns Ok
","created_at":"2026-01-29T16:59:01Z"},{"id":64,"issue_id":"bd-1epw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## REFINEMENT: Backward Compatibility Strategy

### The Breaking Change Problem

Changing `From<u64> for Value` to `TryFrom<u64>` breaks every call site that does `u64_val.into()`. This is a semver-breaking change.

### Recommended Approach: Keep From, Add Warning + Saturating

Instead of removing From entirely, keep it but change behavior:

```rust
impl From<u64> for Value {
    fn from(v: u64) -> Self {
        if v > i64::MAX as u64 {
            // Values exceeding i64::MAX are stored as the maximum i64 value.
            // Use TryFrom<u64> for Value to get an error instead.
            tracing::warn!(
                value = v,
                "u64 value {} exceeds i64::MAX, clamping to i64::MAX. Use TryFrom for strict conversion.",
                v
            );
            Value::BigInt(i64::MAX)
        } else {
            Value::BigInt(v as i64)
        }
    }
}
```

ALSO add TryFrom for users who want strict behavior:
```rust
impl TryFrom<u64> for Value {
    type Error = Error;
    fn try_from(v: u64) -> Result<Self, Self::Error> {
        i64::try_from(v)
            .map(Value::BigInt)
            .map_err(|_| Error::Type(TypeError {
                expected: "u64 within i64 range",
                actual: format!("u64 value {} exceeds i64::MAX ({})", v, i64::MAX),
                column: None,
                rust_type: Some("u64"),
            }))
    }
}
```

This way:
- Existing code using `.into()` keeps working (no breakage)
- The warning makes the issue visible in logs
- Users who need strictness can migrate to `.try_into()?`
- No silent data corruption (clamping is better than wrapping to negative)

### Scope Clarification: Also Fix row.rs

The FromValue trait in row.rs has the reverse conversion (Value → u64). Verify that path also handles overflow correctly. Currently `Value::BigInt(v)` where v is negative should error when converting to u64.

### Testing Note

Test both paths:
- From: verify clamping + warning
- TryFrom: verify error for overflow, Ok for in-range
","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:36:21Z"},{"id":71,"issue_id":"bd-1epw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## SCOPE: This task MUST also verify crates/sqlmodel-core/src/row.rs — the reverse conversion path (Value::BigInt → u64) must handle negative i64 values correctly (should error, not wrap).","created_at":"2026-01-29T17:37:01Z"},{"id":72,"issue_id":"bd-1epw","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## CRITICAL FIX: Cannot have both From<u64> and TryFrom<u64>

The previous refinement proposed keeping `From<u64>` AND adding `TryFrom<u64>`. This is **impossible in Rust** — there's a blanket impl `impl<T, U> TryFrom<U> for T where T: From<U>` so if From exists, TryFrom is auto-derived as infallible and you cannot manually implement it.

### Corrected Approach: Remove From, Add TryFrom + Convenience Method

1. **Remove** `From<u64> for Value`
2. **Add** `TryFrom<u64> for Value` — strict, errors when > i64::MAX
3. **Add** convenience method `Value::from_u64_clamped(v: u64) -> Value` — clamps to i64::MAX with tracing::warn

This gives users:
- `let v: Value = my_u64.try_into()?;` — strict (most common)
- `let v = Value::from_u64_clamped(my_u64);` — lenient with warning

### Breaking Change Mitigation

Yes, removing `From<u64>` breaks `.into()` call sites. But:
- grep shows very few u64→Value conversions in the codebase
- The compiler error is clear and the fix is simple: `.into()` → `.try_into()?`
- Silent wrapping to negative is far worse than a compile error

### Updated Acceptance Criteria
- Remove From<u64> for Value
- Add TryFrom<u64> for Value (errors on overflow)
- Add Value::from_u64_clamped(u64) -> Value (clamps + warns)
- Verify row.rs reverse path (Value::BigInt(negative) → u64 must error)
- Update all call sites in workspace

 {"id":"bd-jrd","title":"Implement UNION, UNION ALL set operations","description":"## Description

Support UNION and UNION ALL to combine query results.

## Python Behavior

```python
from sqlalchemy import union, union_all

# UNION (removes duplicates)
stmt = union(
    select(User).where(User.type == 'admin'),
    select(User).where(User.type == 'superuser')
)

# UNION ALL (keeps duplicates)
stmt = union_all(query1, query2, query3)

result = session.exec(stmt)
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
// Method chaining
let query = select!(User)
    .filter(User::type_.eq("admin"))
    .union(
        select!(User).filter(User::type_.eq("superuser"))
    );

// Function style
let query = union([query1, query2, query3]);
let query = union_all([query1, query2]);
```

### SQL Generation

```sql
(SELECT ... FROM users WHERE type = 'admin')
UNION
(SELECT ... FROM users WHERE type = 'superuser')
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] union() method on Select
- [ ] union_all() method on Select
- [ ] Standalone union(), union_all() functions
- [ ] Works with different SELECT columns (must match)
- [ ] Correct SQL generation for all dialects
- [ ] Can chain multiple unions

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-query/src/set_ops.rs)
- [ ] Test UNION generation
- [ ] Test UNION ALL generation
- [ ] Test column compatibility check
- [ ] Test with ORDER BY/LIMIT

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/query_union.rs)
- [ ] UNION removes duplicates
- [ ] UNION ALL keeps duplicates
- [ ] UNION with different queries
- [ ] UNION with ORDER BY at end
- [ ] UNION subquery in FROM

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: UNION clause SQL generation

 {"id":"bd-kgpu","title":"Implement bulk operations (bulk_insert, bulk_update)","description":"## Description

Efficient bulk insert/update without individual object tracking.

## Python Behavior

```python
# Bulk insert without object tracking
session.bulk_insert_mappings(User, [
    {'name': 'Alice', 'email': 'alice@example.com'},
    {'name': 'Bob', 'email': 'bob@example.com'},
])

# Bulk update
session.bulk_update_mappings(User, [
    {'id': 1, 'name': 'Alice Updated'},
    {'id': 2, 'name': 'Bob Updated'},
])

# Bulk save objects
session.bulk_save_objects([user1, user2, user3])
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
impl Session {
    pub async fn bulk_insert<M: Model>(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
        models: &[M],
    ) -> Outcome<u64, Error> {
        // Single INSERT with multiple rows
        // INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES 
        //   ('Alice', 'alice@...'),
        //   ('Bob', 'bob@...')
    }
    
    pub async fn bulk_update<M: Model>(
        &self,
        cx: &Cx,
        models: &[M],
    ) -> Outcome<u64, Error> {
        // Use CASE WHEN for batch update or multiple statements
    }
}
```

### Optimizations

- Batch into chunks (avoid huge queries)
- Use COPY for PostgreSQL bulk load
- Use LOAD DATA for MySQL

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] bulk_insert with efficient SQL
- [ ] bulk_update with efficient SQL
- [ ] Batch size configurable
- [ ] Returns affected row count
- [ ] Skips object tracking overhead

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-core/src/bulk.rs)
- [ ] Test bulk_insert generates multi-value INSERT
- [ ] Test bulk_update generates CASE/WHEN or multi-UPDATE
- [ ] Test batch size configuration
- [ ] Test empty collection handling
- [ ] Test large batch chunking

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/bulk_operations.rs)
- [ ] bulk_insert 1000 records → single statement
- [ ] bulk_update 1000 records efficiently
- [ ] bulk_insert with returning IDs
- [ ] bulk_insert with FK validation
- [ ] Performance vs individual inserts (10x+ faster)

### Logging
- [ ] INFO: Bulk operation size
- [ ] DEBUG: Batch chunking details
- [ ] TRACE: Individual record processing

-{"id":"bd-kzp1","title":"Implement table inheritance patterns","description":"## Description

Support SQLAlchemy-style table inheritance.

## Single Table Inheritance

All subclasses in one table with discriminator:
```python
class Employee(SQLModel, table=True):
    type: str
    
class Manager(Employee):
    department: str  # Same table, type='manager'
    
class Engineer(Employee):
    specialty: str  # Same table, type='engineer'
```

## Joined Table Inheritance

Each class has own table, FKs link them:
```python
class Person(SQLModel, table=True):
    id: int
    name: str

class Employee(Person, table=True):
    employee_id: int  # FK to person.id
    department: str
```

## Concrete Table Inheritance

Each class is independent table:
```python
class ConcreteEmployee(SQLModel, table=True):
    # All fields repeated, no inheritance at DB level
```

## Rust Implementation

```rust
// Single table
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "single", discriminator = "type")]
struct Employee {
    type_: String,
}

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(inherits = "Employee", discriminator_value = "manager")]
struct Manager {
    department: String,
}

// Joined
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "joined")]
struct Person { ... }

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inherits = "Person")]
struct Employee { ... }
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Single table inheritance
- [ ] Joined table inheritance
- [ ] Concrete table inheritance
- [ ] Discriminator column support
- [ ] Polymorphic queries

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/inheritance.rs)
- [ ] Test single table inheritance
- [ ] Test joined table inheritance
- [ ] Test concrete table inheritance
- [ ] Test discriminator column
- [ ] Test polymorphic queries

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/table_inheritance.rs)
- [ ] Single table: base + derived in same table
- [ ] Joined table: base in one, derived in another with FK
- [ ] Concrete table: each class has own table
- [ ] Query base class returns all subclasses
- [ ] Discriminator column populated correctly

### Logging
- [ ] DEBUG: Inheritance mapping resolution
- [ ] TRACE: Polymorphic query generation
","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:11:59.845508558Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-30T15:27:16.144419981Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-kzp1","depends_on_id":"bd-3lz","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:57:33.834937164Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}],"comments":[{"id":84,"issue_id":"bd-kzp1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented table inheritance parsing and metadata infrastructure:

## Completed:
- Added InheritanceStrategy enum (None, Single, Joined, Concrete) to sqlmodel-core
- Added InheritanceInfo struct for inheritance metadata to sqlmodel-core  
- Added inheritance(), inherits, discriminator_value attribute parsing in sqlmodel-macros
- Added inheritance() method generation in Model trait implementation
- 10 unit tests in sqlmodel-core/field.rs
- 7 parsing tests in sqlmodel-macros/parse.rs  
- 7 integration tests in sqlmodel/lib.rs

## Usage example:
```rust
// Single table inheritance base
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "single")]
struct Employee { type_: String, ... }

// Single table inheritance child
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(inherits = "Employee", discriminator_value = "manager")]  
struct Manager { department: String, ... }

// Joined table inheritance
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "joined")]
struct Person { ... }

#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inherits = "Person")]
struct Student { ... }
```

## Remaining work:
- [ ] Polymorphic query generation (joins for joined inheritance)
- [ ] Discriminator column auto-population
- [ ] E2E tests with actual database operations
- [ ] Schema generation support for inheritance","created_at":"2026-01-30T15:18:13Z"},{"id":85,"issue_id":"bd-kzp1","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Bug fix: Added missing discriminator attribute for struct level.

The original implementation was missing the ability for base models to specify which column is the discriminator. Fixed by:
- Added discriminator_column field to ModelConfigParsed and InheritanceInfo
- Added discriminator attribute parsing (struct level, distinct from field-level discriminator)
- Updated code generation to include discriminator_column
- Added test for discriminator column parsing
- Updated integration test to verify discriminator column is captured

Example usage now complete:
```rust
// Base model specifies discriminator column
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(table, inheritance = "single", discriminator = "type_")]
struct Employee {
    type_: String,
    ...
}

// Child model specifies discriminator value
#[derive(Model)]
#[sqlmodel(inherits = "Employee", discriminator_value = "manager")]
struct Manager { ... }
```","created_at":"2026-01-30T15:27:16Z"}]}

 {"id":"bd-no6","title":"Integrate console into sqlmodel-mysql driver","description":"## Purpose
Add rich console output to the MySQL driver for connection progress, authentication feedback, and query visualization.

## Background
MySQL connections have unique characteristics:
- Capability negotiation (server/client flags)
- Multiple authentication plugins
- Connection attributes
- SHOW commands with formatted output
- Query warnings

## Implementation Details

### File Modifications
crates/sqlmodel-mysql/src/connection.rs
crates/sqlmodel-mysql/src/lib.rs (re-exports)

### ConsoleAware Implementation
Add optional SqlModelConsole to MysqlConnection struct.

### Connection Progress Output
Connecting to MySQL...
  [OK] TCP connected
  [OK] Handshake received (MySQL 8.0.35)
  [OK] Capabilities negotiated
  [..] Authenticating (caching_sha2_password)...
  [OK] Authenticated as user
  [OK] Selected database: myapp
  [OK] Connected (charset: utf8mb4)

### Query Timing and Warnings
Query executed in 8.5ms (10 rows returned)
Warning: Using filesort (consider adding index)

### EXPLAIN Visualization
Parse and display EXPLAIN output:
- Show query plan as tree
- Highlight full table scans
- Show index usage
- Display estimated vs actual rows

### SHOW Commands Formatting
Format common SHOW outputs nicely:
SHOW TABLES:
  - users (InnoDB, 10,234 rows)
  - posts (InnoDB, 45,678 rows)
  - sessions (MEMORY, 123 rows)

SHOW PROCESSLIST:
+----+------+-----------+------+---------+------+-------+
| Id | User | Host      | db   | Command | Time | State |
+----+------+-----------+------+---------+------+-------+
...

### Replication Status
For SHOW REPLICA STATUS:
- Lag indicator
- Position info
- Error highlighting

## Plain Text Mode
Connected to MySQL 8.0.35 at localhost:3306
Query: 8.5ms, 10 rows
Warning: Using filesort

## Conditional Compilation
Use cfg feature flag same as other drivers.

## Verification Steps
1. Test connection with console enabled
2. Test different auth plugins
3. Test EXPLAIN visualization
4. Test SHOW command formatting
5. Verify warning display
6. Test plain text output
7. Test with console disabled

## Dependencies
- sqlmodel-console crate
- SqlModelConsole, Theme components","acceptance_criteria":"MySQL driver implements ConsoleAware trait
Connection events emit console output
Query execution outputs to console when enabled
Error states display through ErrorPanel
All unit tests verify console integration
Integration tests verify end-to-end output","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-19T21:13:51.553120476Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-21T21:17:26.391457199Z","closed_at":"2026-01-21T21:17:26.025681677Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-no6","depends_on_id":"bd-1lv","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-no6","depends_on_id":"bd-2kb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-no6","depends_on_id":"bd-88i","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:53:31Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":28,"issue_id":"bd-no6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Unit Tests to Implement

All tests pass.","created_at":"2026-01-21T21:17:26Z"}]}
 {"id":"bd-ntpn","title":"Implement @computed_field equivalent","description":"## Description

Support computed fields that derive value from other fields.

## Python Behavior

```python
from pydantic import computed_field

class User(SQLModel):
    first_name: str
    last_name: str
    
    @computed_field
    @property
    def full_name(self) -> str:
        return f'{self.first_name} {self.last_name}'
```

## Behavior

- Computed at access time
- Included in model_dump() by default
- Can be excluded with exclude_computed_fields=True
- NOT stored in database

## Rust Implementation

### Option 1: Macro attribute
```rust
#[derive(Model)]
struct User {
    first_name: String,
    last_name: String,
    
    #[sqlmodel(computed = "compute_full_name")]
    #[serde(skip_deserializing)]
    full_name: String,
}

impl User {
    fn compute_full_name(&self) -> String {
        format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name)
    }
}
```

### Option 2: Separate trait
```rust
impl ComputedFields for User {
    type Computed = UserComputed;
    
    fn compute(&self) -> UserComputed {
        UserComputed {
            full_name: format!("{} {}", self.first_name, self.last_name),
        }
    }
}
```

## Serialization

Generated serde:
```rust
impl Serialize for User {
    fn serialize<S>(&self, serializer: S) -> Result<S::Ok, S::Error> {
        // Include computed fields in output
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Computed fields can be declared
- [ ] Value computed from method
- [ ] Included in serialization
- [ ] Excluded from database columns
- [ ] Can exclude from dump with option

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests (crates/sqlmodel-macros/src/computed.rs)
- [ ] Test computed field declaration
- [ ] Test computed method is called
- [ ] Test computed included in serialization
- [ ] Test computed excluded from DB columns
- [ ] Test exclude_computed_fields option

### E2E Tests (tests/e2e/computed_fields.rs)
- [ ] first_name + last_name → full_name computed
- [ ] Computed field in JSON output
- [ ] Computed field NOT in INSERT SQL
- [ ] model_dump(exclude_computed_fields=True) works
- [ ] Computed depends on other computed (chaining)

### Logging
- [ ] TRACE: Computed field evaluation
","notes":"DarkStone claiming to work on completing computed fields. Will implement: (1) computed method specification, (2) compute method calls, (3) exclude_computed_fields filtering in model_dump.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T05:09:12.184930028Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T18:14:16.469397176Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T18:14:16.469330001Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ntpn","depends_on_id":"bd-1fs","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-28T16:57:43.589089577Z","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
